sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: A population of insects was studied after a pesticide was introduced. Before exposure, genotype frequencies at a detoxification locus approximately matched Hardy-Weinberg expectations. After several generations, the aa genotype increased in frequency and survived pesticide exposure better than AA or Aa. Mating observations did not show strong mate preference by genotype.
questions.stem: Which evolutionary force best explains the increase in aa frequency after pesticide exposure?

choices.A: Natural selection favoring pesticide-resistant aa individuals
choices.B: Random mating with no fitness differences
choices.C: Mutation converting every A allele to a chromosome

explanations.A: Correct. The aa genotype survived pesticide exposure better, so pesticide imposed selection favoring aa.
explanations.B: Random mating alone would not systematically increase a genotype with higher survival.
explanations.C: The passage indicates survival differences, not universal mutation.
explanations.D: Meiosis did not stop; generations continued.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Genotype frequencies before and after pesticide selection.

questions.stem: If the initial population has q^2 = 0.09 for aa, what is q?

explanations.D: Correct. The recessive genotype frequency is q^2, so q = sqrt(0.09) = 0.30.
explanations.B: 0.49 corresponds to p^2 in the example.

questions.stem: The observation of no strong mate preference argues against which Hardy-Weinberg violation?

explanations.B: Correct. Mate preference by genotype would be nonrandom mating; the observations do not support that as the main cause.
explanations.A: The statement does not rule out drift by population size.
explanations.C: Selection is directly supported by survival differences.
explanations.D: The statement about mate preference does not address mutation.

questions.stem: If heterozygotes have intermediate survival, the aa allele is best described as:

choices.C: Partially dominant with respect to resistance only if Aa shows some resistance
choices.D: A protein with no genetic basis

explanations.C: Correct. Intermediate heterozygote phenotype would indicate incomplete dominance or additive effects for resistance.
explanations.A: The aa genotype is present and increases.
explanations.B: The locus is described with AA/Aa/aa nuclear-style genotypes.
explanations.D: Alleles are genetic variants that can encode protein differences.

questions.stem: Which outcome would most weaken the selection interpretation?

choices.D: All genotypes survived pesticide exposure equally well
choices.C: The detoxification locus encoded an enzyme

explanations.D: Correct. If all genotypes had equal survival, differential fitness would not explain genotype-frequency change.
explanations.B: Random mating does not weaken evidence for survival-based selection.
explanations.C: An enzyme locus could plausibly mediate detoxification.

questions.passage: Researchers mapped four linked genes by measuring recombinant offspring after test crosses. Recombination frequency increased with estimated distance between genes, but the largest distances underestimated physical separation because multiple crossovers sometimes restored parental marker combinations.
questions.stem: What does a low recombination frequency between two genes generally indicate?

choices.A: The genes are close together on the same chromosome
choices.B: The genes are on separate chromosomes only
choices.C: The genes are translated by the same ribosome at the same time
choices.D: The genes have identical DNA sequences

explanations.A: Correct. Genes close together are less likely to have a crossover occur between them.
explanations.B: Genes on separate chromosomes assort independently and approach 50% recombination.
explanations.C: Ribosomal translation is unrelated to meiotic recombination frequency.
explanations.D: Linked genes need not have identical sequences.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Relationship between gene distance and recombination frequency.

questions.stem: Why can recombination frequency underestimate distance at large separations?

choices.B: Multiple crossovers can restore parental marker combinations
choices.C: All linked genes recombine at exactly 100%

explanations.B: Correct. Double crossovers can make offspring appear nonrecombinant for outer markers, causing underestimation.
explanations.A: Crossovers do occur in eukaryotic meiosis.
explanations.C: Recombination frequencies max out near 50%, not 100%.
explanations.D: Meiosis segregates chromosomes; it does not translate RNA into proteins.

questions.stem: A test cross commonly uses an individual heterozygous at linked loci crossed with:

choices.A: An unrelated species with no homologous chromosomes

explanations.C: Correct. A homozygous recessive tester makes recombinant and parental gametes from the heterozygote easier to identify phenotypically.
explanations.A: Homologous chromosomes are needed for standard genetic crosses.
explanations.B: Cells lacking DNA cannot provide genetic markers.
explanations.D: Ribosomes are not organisms used in crosses.

questions.stem: Which gene pair in the figure is likely farthest apart among the listed pairs?

explanations.A: Correct. C-D has the highest plotted recombination frequency and estimated distance among the listed pairs.
explanations.B: A-B has the lowest distance/frequency shown.
explanations.D: 0% recombination would suggest extremely tight linkage or no recombination in the sample.

questions.stem: A recombination frequency near 50% suggests that two loci are:

choices.D: Unlinked or so far apart that they assort approximately independently
choices.A: Always inherited together with no crossovers

explanations.D: Correct. A 50% recombination frequency is the maximum expected for independent assortment or very distant loci.
explanations.A: Always inherited together would produce near 0% recombination.
explanations.B: Recombination frequency is not a direct count of base pairs.
explanations.C: The same codon would not produce 50% recombinant offspring.
